steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The sentence reads: "The gaseous ______ have molar masses of 42.08 and 44.10 g/mol, respectively." The blank contains "compounds" followed by "propylene and propane," which specifies which compounds. Rule: a restrictive appositive (one that identifies which specific thing is meant) takes no commas. A non-restrictive appositive (extra, removable information) takes commas on both sides.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
A. compounds, propylene and propane, — commas on both sides treat "propylene and propane" as non-restrictive (removable extra info). Reconstruct: "The gaseous compounds have molar masses of 42.08 and 44.10 g/mol" — but without the names we don't know which compounds. The appositive is essential here. Eliminated.

B. compounds propylene and propane — no commas. Restrictive treatment. Reconstruct: "The gaseous compounds propylene and propane have molar masses of 42.08 and 44.10 g/mol, respectively." Grammatically correct; "propylene and propane" specifies exactly which gaseous compounds. Correct.

C. compounds propylene and propane. — a period mid-sentence creates a sentence fragment ("have molar masses..." has no subject). Eliminated.

D. compounds, propylene and propane — comma before but not after creates an asymmetric, unpaired insertion. Reconstruct: "The gaseous compounds, propylene and propane have molar masses..." — the missing closing comma makes the sentence structurally broken. Eliminated.
